name: Omega-3 (fish oil) for cardiovascular events
summary: Whether fish oil prevents cardiovascular events depends heavily on the preparation and population. High-dose EPA-only trials (REDUCE-IT, JELIS) and the new hemodialysis trial (PISCES) show clear benefit, while low-dose or EPA+DHA combination trials (VITAL, ASCEND, STRENGTH) are essentially null. A pooled average therefore hides more than it reveals.
description: Several large randomized trials have tested marine omega-3 fatty acids for prevention of major cardiovascular events, with famously divergent results. The trials differ in formulation (purified EPA vs EPA+DHA), dose (about 1 g/day vs 4 g/day), comparator oil (mineral, corn, olive), and population (primary prevention vs high-risk vs hemodialysis). PISCES (2026) is the newest and tested 4 g/day fish oil specifically in patients receiving hemodialysis.
interpretation:
Do not read the pooled diamond as the answer — the heterogeneity here is the point, and it is very high. The trials split by formulation and dose more than by "fish oil yes/no": the positive trials used high-dose EPA-only preparations (REDUCE-IT 4 g/day icosapent ethyl; JELIS 1.8 g/day purified EPA), whereas trials using ~1 g/day (VITAL, ASCEND) or high-dose EPA+DHA (STRENGTH) were null. STRENGTH is the sharpest contrast: same 4 g/day dose as REDUCE-IT but EPA+DHA, and flatly null.
Three honest confounders: comparator oil differed (REDUCE-IT used mineral oil, which raised LDL/CRP in the control arm and may have inflated the apparent benefit; STRENGTH used corn oil; ASCEND olive oil); JELIS was open-label in a high-fish-intake Japanese population; and the endpoint definitions are not identical across trials.
PISCES is the only trial here in hemodialysis patients, and its headline was a recurrent-events analysis (HR 0.57); the value shown is the participant-level risk ratio (≥1 serious cardiovascular event), which is more comparable to the other trials' first-event endpoints but still not strictly like-for-like. See the glossary for hazard ratio, heterogeneity, and composite outcomes.
methodologyNotes: Risk ratios are computed from each trial's reported participant-level primary-event counts (2x2) for REDUCE-IT (705/4089 vs 901/4090), STRENGTH (785/6539 vs 795/6539), ASCEND (689/7740 vs 712/7740), JELIS (262/9326 vs 324/9319), and PISCES (127/610 vs 208/618). VITAL is entered as its reported hazard ratio 0.92 (0.80-1.06) with a control risk for NNT; GISSI-Prevenzione as a risk ratio 0.90 (0.82-0.99) from its reported relative risk reduction (per-arm counts not confirmed). Computed risk ratios are very close to the published hazard ratios. Pooling is shown for completeness but is of limited interpretive value given the formulation/dose/population/comparator differences and the resulting high heterogeneity (I²); the comparison is best read trial by trial, especially high-dose EPA vs the rest. PISCES's headline endpoint was recurrent events (HR 0.57); the participant-level risk ratio is used here for comparability.

outcomes.label: Major cardiovascular events
outcomes.description: Each trial's primary cardiovascular composite endpoint (definitions vary).

studies.author: PISCES (Lok)
studies.citation: Lok CE, Farkouh ME, Hemmelgarn BR, et al. Fish-Oil Supplementation and Cardiovascular Events in Patients Receiving Hemodialysis (PISCES). N Engl J Med. 2026;394:128-137.
studies.doseRegimen: Fish oil 4 g/day (1.6 g EPA + 0.8 g DHA)
studies.notes: Participants with ≥1 serious cardiovascular event. Headline primary (recurrent events) was HR 0.57 (0.47-0.70). Only dialysis trial here; serious bleeding was lower with fish oil (4.8% vs 7.6%).

studies.author: REDUCE-IT (Bhatt)
studies.citation: Bhatt DL, Steg PG, Miller M, et al. Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Icosapent Ethyl for Hypertriglyceridemia (REDUCE-IT). N Engl J Med. 2019;380:11-22.
studies.notes: Positive. Published HR 0.75 (0.68-0.83). Mineral-oil comparator raised LDL/CRP in controls (debated).

studies.author: JELIS (Yokoyama)
studies.citation: Yokoyama M, Origasa H, Matsuzaki M, et al. Effects of eicosapentaenoic acid on major coronary events in hypercholesterolaemic patients (JELIS). Lancet. 2007;369:1090-1098.
studies.notes: Positive. Published HR 0.81 (0.69-0.95). Open-label (PROBE) design; high-fish-intake population.

studies.author: GISSI-Prevenzione
studies.citation: GISSI-Prevenzione Investigators. Dietary supplementation with n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin E after myocardial infarction (GISSI-Prevenzione). Lancet. 1999;354:447-455.
studies.notes: Modestly positive. RR from reported relative risk reduction ~10% (two-way analysis); per-arm counts not confirmed. Open-label.

studies.author: VITAL (Manson)
studies.citation: Manson JE, Cook NR, Lee IM, et al. Marine n-3 Fatty Acids and Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease and Cancer (VITAL). N Engl J Med. 2019;380:23-32.
studies.notes: Null for the primary composite (MI/stroke/CV death); MI alone was lower (HR 0.72, 0.59-0.90).

studies.author: ASCEND
studies.citation: ASCEND Study Collaborative Group. Effects of n-3 Fatty Acid Supplements in Diabetes Mellitus (ASCEND). N Engl J Med. 2018;379:1540-1550.

studies.author: STRENGTH (Nicholls)
studies.citation: Nicholls SJ, Lincoff AM, Garcia M, et al. Effect of High-Dose Omega-3 Fatty Acids vs Corn Oil on Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (STRENGTH). JAMA. 2020;324:2268-2280.
studies.notes: Null (HR 0.99, 0.90-1.09). Same 4 g/day dose as REDUCE-IT but EPA+DHA — the key contrast.
